sections.text: We have already met clients, servers, DNS, and HTTP. Now let's put it all together and see the full journey a single click takes, from the moment you ask for a page to the moment it appears on your screen.

sections.title: What is the Request-Response Cycle?
sections.text: The request-response cycle is the complete round trip that happens every time your browser asks for something and gets an answer back. It is called a cycle because it repeats over and over, every single time you click a link, load a page, or press a button that needs new information.

sections.title: Why It Matters
sections.text: Almost everything that happens on the web follows this same basic pattern: something asks, and something answers. Once you understand this cycle, you will recognize it everywhere, whether you are loading a webpage, sending a message, or even watching a video online.

sections.title: The Full Journey of a Web Request
items: You type a URL or click a link in your browser.
Your browser uses DNS to find the IP address of the right server.
Your browser sends an HTTP request to that server, asking for a page.
The server receives the request, prepares the page, and sends back an HTTP response.
Your browser receives the response and turns it into the page you see, ready for you to read or click again.

sections.title: Think of it like Mailing a Letter and Getting a Reply
sections.text: You write a letter asking a question and mail it to a friend. Your friend reads it, writes an answer, and mails it back to you. You open the reply and now you have what you asked for. The request-response cycle works the same way, just much, much faster.
A single webpage can actually trigger many request-response cycles at once, one for the HTML, others for images, styles, and scripts, all happening together in the background.

terms.definition: A message sent by the client asking the server for something.
The message the server sends back, containing what was asked for.
A process that repeats, in this case the repeating pattern of asking and answering.
The full journey of a request going out and a response coming back.
The complete process of a client asking for something and a server answering it.

options: The complete process of a client asking for something and a server answering
questions.explanation: The request-response cycle is the full round trip where a client sends a request and a server sends back a response.

questions.text: What happens right after your browser finds the server's IP address using DNS?
options: The browser sends an HTTP request to that server
questions.explanation: Once the browser knows the IP address from DNS, it sends an HTTP request to that server asking for the page.

questions.text: Can one webpage trigger more than one request-response cycle?
options: No, only one is ever allowed
Yes, separate cycles can happen for the HTML, images, and other files
Only if you refresh the page twice
questions.explanation: A single webpage often loads many things separately, like images and styles, each one using its own request-response cycle.
